transcript.text: Before we get started, we'd love to introduce you to my colleague, Sam Mita.

Nice to meet you both.
Nice to meet you.

Nice to meet you, too.
Nice to meet you.

Privacy policies are usually good.
by Dr.
Thank
for about six to 12 months, and they need to be future-facing for about that long.

And DPAs tend to have a longer shelf life just because they're slightly broader.

be...

still figuring out like licensing and working with your patent team as well and stuff, so that one's like not as baked, but this one is launching soon, so we'll focus on that.

But, yeah, yeah, that's, that's it.

you.

name, email address, maybe phone number if they enroll it in MFA, you know, company, and maybe a job title or something like that is what we would get from the users.

with GDPR or startups.

Free services do make that a little bit awkward.
So structurally, one way that we could do it, because again, your privacy policy won't apply to B2B use.
We could say, for the logged out experience, right, before you go through the order flows, agree to any terms, the privacy policy applies.
Right?
Which, there are pros and cons, we can talk about that.

Or would you like me to talk through a little bit?

Yeah, that's, Bridget, correct me if I'm wrong, but that's, like, most of the users, that's what we want.
Now, we will bundle this, or we may bundle this with services or things like that, and so we'll, you know, give them a subscription for a duration or something like that.
But, you know, I think most of our users, we want to be able to go through a self-service funnel.
Okay, that's super helpful.
I'm going to give you a...

And you let me know if it doesn't map, but I think then if self-service sort of prosumer users that we treat as paying businesses after they convert, I propose we draft a privacy policy that basically says, if you're using our website, any services as part of our logged out experience, privacy policy attaches.
Because we don't know, we don't know who you are, we don't know what you do, but any information you submit to us, we're going to process it as the controller.
However, once you do create a paying account, we treat you as a business, and our terms will apply, including any sort of data processing terms.

Then the decision that has to be made at this fork in the road is whether you want your DPA to be referenced by default in your terms with customers, or whether you want customers to have to ask you for a DPA.
Pros and cons.
Used to be that you as the processor in the U.S.
did not have to provide it.
Still most of the case.
In the EU, processors have an independent obligation to provide a DPA.
So I would say the easier thing for you to do is just say, if you're a business customer, you get our DPA.
Easy peasy.
Does that ring for you or do you rather segment it a little bit?
That sounds about right to me, particularly since like, you there's all kinds of like, I'll call it 2BPR adjacent laws these days.
You know, CCPA, CPRA, and the funding market we solve on and, you know, those.
And so, probably the easiest way to go.
Now, the other twist to this is...

I think we own all content in the platform, so if they say give us a prompt and then we generate stuff for it, we can reuse that prompt, we can reuse all the data we present to them, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera, cetera.
Because, you know, we, you know, if they tell us a new category, that's now in our category list that we present to other customers, right?
So I'll weigh in on this from a privacy perspective, and then I'll defer to Sam from an IP perspective.
If you do agree to a DPA with all customers, one of the primary restrictions on processing that you agree to is with respect to personal information.
You're only going to use it for the purpose of that one customer, and you won't commingle it with personal information you gather from other customers.
What makes that tricky is...
If PII is in the prompts or in the outputs, you would need some sort of filtering mechanism to actually comply with your DPA.
What a lot of businesses in your situation do is something like named entity replacement.
So if it spits out, I don't know, Bridget as an example of a technical operator, you would just swap that out to Purple7.
You know, and that way you can still use the prompt from a privacy perspective without violating your DPA.
And yeah, I'll pass it to Sam on the IP front.
Sam, not to put you on the spot.
The thing that I'm thinking about is like, there is a fundamental decision from an IP perspective about owning inputs and outputs versus licensing.
So I'll let you speak to that.
I think typically we recommend that our clients like own

It sounds like you're intending a lot of people to just come onto your website, go through the click-through process, and so there's not a lot of negotiation.

So, you know, depending on how sophisticated your future customers are, I don't know if they're going to be reading through everything, but we can absolutely put language in that says that GrowthX will own all inputs, that you'll be able to use it for training of your own systems.
Just want to flag that if someone sees it, they might want to, you know, engage with your sales team or someone, because we do see that that is one of the more negotiated points.
